title-group.article-title: Cancer risk in patients with recurrent or persistent symptoms: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aQueen Mary University of London, Centre for Cancer Screening, Prevention and Early Diagnosis, Wolfson Institute of Population Health, Charterhouse Square, London, EC1M 6BT, UK
bUniversity of Aberdeen, School of Medicine, Medical Sciences and Nutrition, Institute of Applied Health Sciences, Academic Primary Care, Aberdeen, AB25 2ZD, UK
cBarts Health NHS Trust, The Royal London Hospital, Whitechapel Rd, London, E1 1BB, UK
dImperial College London, Department of Immunology and Inflammation, Commonwealth Building, The Hammersmith Hospital, London, W12 0NN, UK
eKings College London, Strand, London, WC2R 2LS, UK

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).

abstract.title: Summary
abstract.p: Most cancers are diagnosed following symptomatic presentation. European guidelines supporting general practitioner cancer referrals largely focus on ‘alarm’ symptoms. However, about half of cancer patients do not present with alarm symptoms. Understanding cancer risk in these patients can help identify those who might need further investigations. This systematic review quantified positive predictive values (PPVs) of recurrent and persistent symptoms for cancer. Evidence from 21 studies reporting on nearly 200,000 patients, identified PPVs for 33 recurrent and 22 persistent symptoms. ‘Persistent’ and ‘recurrent’ symptoms were inconsistently defined. In addition to recurrent ‘alarm’ symptoms, recurrent abdominal distension, abdominal pain and rectal bleeding had PPVs above 3%, the existing English referral threshold, in patients younger than currently recommended for specialist investigation. Persistent symptoms were predominately studied in referred populations. Clearer definitions of recurrence and persistence, and prospective validation of cancer risk for patients with non-alarm symptoms across all age groups are needed to refine guidance and support early cancer diagnosis.

list-item.p: Most guidelines for suspected cancer in Europe are based on alarm symptoms, but almost half of cancer patients do not present with these. Many present with recurring or persistent symptoms which have lower risk for cancer.

We aimed to quantify positive predictive values (PPVs) of symptoms which are recurrent or persistent for cancer, focusing on symptoms with PPVs of 3% or more, the current threshold used in the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines in England and Wales.
Evidence was limited for most cancer sites, with the maximum number of studies per site being five, for colorectal cancer.
We found lack of evidence on the definitions of the time intervals used to define ‘recurrence’ and on ‘persistent’ symptoms.
Recurrent presentation of alarm symptoms was associated with the highest PPVs for cancer.
Recurrent abdominal pain and rectal bleeding in age groups younger than currently recommended in NICE guidelines had PPVs of 3% or more.
Future research should focus on validating PPVs for recurrent and persistent symptoms in large-scale prospective studies, especially in younger patients and those with abdominal symptoms.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cancer is the second leading cause of death worldwide, with nearly 10 million deaths in 2022.1 The World Health Organization predicts that by 2050, global cancer rates will increase by 77% due to an ageing population and ongoing exposure to risk factors such as smoking, alcohol, obesity and pollution.2 In the United Kingdom (UK), the National Health Service (NHS) Long Term Plan (2019) aimed to diagnose 75% of cancers at an early stage (Stage 1 or 2) by 2028.3 Despite the updated NHS 10-Year Plan (2025) placing greater emphasis on prevention, most cancer patients are diagnosed following symptomatic presentations4 following referrals by general practitioners (GPs), with only 6% diagnosed via national screening programmes.5 Timely referral from primary care is therefore crucial for early-stage diagnosis, which enables more curative treatments and improved cancer survivals.4,5 Equipping GPs with the right tools to triage undifferentiated presentations and identify higher-risk symptomatic patients for prompt referral can facilitate policy initiatives that aim to improve cancer diagnosis and outcomes.

Many European countries have national guidelines to support GPs in referring symptomatic patients with suspected cancer. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline NG126 on suspected cancer provides GPs in England and Wales with recommendations for referring people who have symptoms associated with cancer risk of ≥3%. Similar national guidelines exist elsewhere, including Scotland, Ireland, Denmark and Norway.7, 8, 9 These guidance focus largely on single high-risk (‘alarm’) symptoms or combinations of symptoms with risk factors, such as smoking and age.6,10 However, evidence from Denmark suggests that around half of cancer patients do not present with alarm symptoms9,11 and up to 10% of adult patients present with recurrent or persistent symptoms in primary care.12 These symptoms may occur as non-specific symptoms, and may not be immediately attributed to cancer.13 Many patients also present multiple times before a cancer diagnosis is made.14 Understanding cancer risk associated with recurrent and persistent symptoms is critical for early cancer diagnosis in some of the 50% of patients not presenting with alarm symptoms. With the increasing pressure on secondary care and an ageing population, it is important to identify higher-risk patients for referral from primary care while also reassuring people at lower risk of cancer who do not immediately need further investigations.

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review to quantify the positive predictive values (PPVs) of recurrent or persistent symptoms for cancer diagnosis in primary care. We also aimed to examine how recurrence and persistence are defined across different study designs and data sources. From our clinical experience, we were interested in repeated presentations with the same symptoms (‘recurrent’) and symptoms which were described as ‘persistent’ by authors. We did not further operationalise these concepts to capture the breadth of the evidence available. Due to prior knowledge that much of the relevant evidence was generated in the UK, we sought to identify recurrent or persistent symptoms which had PPVs of ≥3% for cancer, in line with the current referral threshold used in the NICE NG12 guidelines and using it as a pragmatic and policy-relevant reference point.6

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: At least two independent reviewers (AA, AC, AV, FD, MM, NC, YZ) extracted data using a standardised form which included first author, publication year, country, cancer site, study design, study setting, population, sample size, recurrent or persistent symptoms and their definitions, and diagnostic performance statistics. Two reviewers (FD, NC) independently verified the data on the performance statistics extracted for all included studies.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: We selected PPVs as the main diagnostic performance measure of interest, due to its direct relevance to NICE NG12 guidelines using a ≥3% PPV threshold. Narrative synthesis was used to present the findings.

We used the Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies (QUADAS-2) tool15 to evaluate the risk of bias and applicability of included studies. QUADAS-2 comprises four domains: patient selection, index test (recurrent/persistent symptoms), reference standard, and flow and timing. At least two reviewers (AA, AC, AV, FD, MM, NC, YZ) assessed all included studies for risk of bias. To minimise inconsistencies across assessments, specific assessment criteria were developed (Supplementary Table S2), informed by official guidance, clinical expertise, and comments from the initial assessment. The revised criteria were then applied, and disagreements were solved by consensus (NC and YZ).

sec.title: Role of funding source
sec.p: There was no funding source for this study.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Database searches retrieved 24,445 publications, with 17,766 titles and abstracts screened for eligibility. Full-text screening of 392 articles resulted in 33 included publications. Citation searches identified six additional articles, resulting in 39 included studies. Twenty-one of these reported data on recurrent or persistent symptoms and were included in this analysis (Supplementary File S3).

Most studies were conducted in the UK (n = 16), two in Sweden, and one each in Denmark, Iran and Canada (Table 1). Studies were published between 2005 and 2023 and included 16 case–control, two retrospective cohort, one cross-sectional, one prospective survey and one mixed (prospective and prospective) cohort designs. A total of 194,964 patients, including 35,807 cancer patients, were included in the studies, with individual study sample sizes ranging from 1079 (minimum) to 40,348 (maximum), with 47–7471 cancer patients. Most studies were conducted in primary care (n = 16).Table 1
Characteristics of included studies.
Lead author (year)CountryDesign (as described by authors)Design (as classified by review team)Sett-ingCancer typeStudy populationPopulation for whom PPVs were availableOverall sample sizeCancer caseNo. and type of symptom(s) reported in each study
Dyrop 201416Denmarkreview of medical recordsretrospective cohortSCSarcoma (bone and soft tissue)tertiary referrals–patients with suspected sarcomaall ages11262582 persistent
Ewing 201617Swedentotal population-based case–controlcase–controlPCGI (CRC)≥18 y diagnosed with CRC≥50 y25815425 recurrent

Hamilton 200620UKpopulation based case–controlcase–controlPCGU (prostate)≥40 y diagnosed with prostate cancer≥40 y12972175 recurrent
Hamilton 200921UKpopulation based case–controlcase–controlPCGynaecological (ovarian)≥40 y diagnosed with ovarian cancer≥40 y12722125 recurrent
Khademi 201222Irancross-sectionalcross-sectionalSCGI (upper GI)GI clinic referrals–patients presenting with dyspepsiaall ages, ≤35 y, 36–49 y, 50–64 y and ≥65 y2847871 persistent
McKie 200823UKretrospective auditretrospective cohortSCH&N (lymphoma, oral, lung and laryngeal cancer)fast track H&N cancer pathway referrals16–101 y10791185 persistent
Nemlander 202324Swedenpopulation-based matched case–controlcase–controlPCGI (CRC)≥18 y diagnosed with CRC≥18 y (males, females and combined), ≥50 y and ≥70 y14,54829205 recurrent
Shephard 201225UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCGU (bladder)≥40 y diagnosed with bladder cancer≥60 y26,63349155 recurrent
Shephard 201326UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCGU (kidney)≥40 y diagnosed with kidney cancer≥60 y18,89031497 recurrent
Shephard 2015a27UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCHaematological (HL)≥40 y diagnosed with HL≥60 y15202831 persistent
Shephard 2015b28UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCHaematological (NHL)≥40 y diagnosed with NHL40–59 y and ≥60 y23,83043621 recurrent & 2 persistent
Shephard 201829UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCGU (testicular)≥16 y diagnosed with testicular cancer<50 y635413982 recurrent
Shephard 201930UKmatched case–control study (GPRD)case–controlPCH&N (laryngeal cancer)≥40 y diagnosed with laryngeal cancer≥60 y43658063 recurrent
Simpkins 201631Canadanot specifiedprospective surveySCGI (CRC)patients presenting with lower GI symptomsall ages (16–93 y) persistent; ≥40 y, <50 y pairs1981474 persistent
Stapley 201232UKcase–control (GPRD)case–controlPCGI (pancreas)≥40 y diagnosed with pancreatic cancer>60 y20,09436353 recurrent
Stapley 201333UKcase–control (GPRD)case–controlPCGI (upper GI)≥40 y diagnosed with oesophageogastric cancer>55 y40,34874713 recurrent
Stapley 201734UKobservational, prospective (CPRD)case–controlPCGI (CRC)18–49 y diagnosed with CRC or inflammatory bowel disease18–49 y564016616 recurrent
Tikka 201635UKnot specifiedmixed (prospective and retrospective cohort)SCH&N (throat, oral, pharynx, larynx, thyroid, secondary cancer)fast track H&N cancer pathway referrals16–102 y471539712 persistent
Walker 201336UKcase–control (GPRD)case–controlPCGynaecological (endometrial–excluding cervical)≥40 y diagnosed with uterine cancer≥55 y12,26927322 recurrent
CPRD, Clinical Practice Research Database; CRC, colorectal cancer; GI, gastrointestinal; GPRD, General Practice Research Database; GU, urogenital; H&N, head & neck cancer; HL, Hodgkin lymphoma; NHL, non-Hodgkin lymphoma; No., number; PC, primary care; SC, secondary care; UK, United Kingdom; y, years.

Almost all studies reported on specified cancer sites (n = 19). The most common cancer sites were: colorectal (n = 5), urogenital (bladder, kidney, prostate, testicular) (n = 4), upper gastrointestinal (GI) (upper GI, pancreatic) (n = 3), haematological (non-Hodgkin and Hodgkin lymphoma) (n = 2), and one study each for endometrial, laryngeal, lung, ovarian, and sarcoma. The other two studies reported on multiple cancer sites in the head and neck area. All studies reported on adult populations.

Fourteen studies reported on PPVs for recurrent symptoms only, six for persistent symptoms only and one reported PPVs for both recurrent and persistent symptoms (Table 1). Across all studies, 85 PPVs for 33 unique recurrent symptoms, and 44 PPVs for 22 unique persistent symptoms were reported.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p:
There was wide variation in risk of bias across all four domains (Table 2). Risk of bias for patient selection was high (n = 11) or unclear (n = 6) for most studies, due to the high number of case–control studies (n = 16; 76%). Risk of bias for the index test (symptom of interest) was mostly low (n = 16; 76%); in all other cases (n = 5; 34%) this was deemed unclear due to limited information on whether those collecting symptom data were aware of the patient diagnosis or the extent to which symptom data had to be recoded or interpreted. Risk of bias for the reference standard (‘gold standard’ diagnosis) was unclear (n = 13; 62%) or low (n = 8; 38%), and for flow and timing it was mostly unclear (n = 19; 90%). Main causes for uncertainty were lack of information on the reference standard, and patients in case–control studies not receiving any reference standard. In contrast, there were low applicability concerns across all three domains.Table 2
QUADAS-2 risk of bias assessment results.

sec.title: Recurrent symptoms
sec.p: All studies reporting on symptom recurrence were conducted in primary care (n = 15), of case–control design, and from the UK excepting two studies.17,24 Most case–control studies (14/16) had the same author either as a first or last author (Hamilton), and ten studies used the Clinical Practice Research Datalink or its former version (General Practice Research Database).37 The studies reported on 14 cancer sites, with the most frequent site (colorectal) reported in five studies.

Symptom information was obtained from medical codes, with recurrence defined as at least two attendances with the same coded symptom(s) in a pre-defined study period. Twelve studies examined up to one year, and three studies included data up to two years pre-diagnosis. However, the interval between repeat presentations was not reported in any study (Supplementary Table S4).

Most studies reporting on recurrent symptoms included participants aged ≥40 years, except for Stapley 201734 (18–49 years) and Nemlander 202324 (≥18–≥70 years). PPVs for symptom recurrence were often reported specifically for older age groups within the included research participants, a decision often justified by the authors due to cancer risk increasing with age.

The most frequently reported recurrent symptoms were abdominal pain (n = 13), weight loss (n = 8), bleeding (n = 8), change in bowel habit (CIBH) (n = 7), and anaemia (n = 6). The cancer site with the highest number of reported recurrent symptoms was colorectal cancer. These include four studies reporting on recurrent abdominal pain/tenderness, anaemia, bleeding, CIBH, constipation, diarrhoea, nausea/vomiting, rectal bleeding and weight loss.

PPVs for recurrent symptoms ranged from 0.03% for shortness of breath (laryngeal cancer, ≥60 years) to 31.6% for jaundice (pancreatic cancer, ≥60 years) (Table 3). Symptoms with PPVs ≥3% included (in descending order): jaundice, haemoptysis (17%, lung cancer patients ≥40 years), post-menopausal bleeding (9.6%, endometrial cancer patients ≥55 years), bleeding/rectal bleeding (range 3.5%–7.8% varying by age groups, colorectal cancer), visible haematuria (6.1%, bladder cancer, patients ≥40 years), dysphagia (5.5%, upper GI cancer patients >55 years), abdominal distension (4.3%, ovarian cancer patients ≥40 years), nocturia (3.3%) and urinary frequency/urgency (3.1%) for prostate cancer patients ≥40 years, and abdominal pain (3.0%, colorectal cancer patients ≥40 years).Table 3
Positive Predictive Values (PPVs) for recurrent symptoms, by cancer site.
PPV (CI)
Bladder cancer
Shephard 2012a; ≥60 years old
Visible haematuria6.1% (5.1–8.2)
Dysuria1.0% (0.7–1.5)
Urinary Tract Infection0.5% (0.4–0.6)
Abdominal pain0.2% (0.1–0.2)

CI, Confidence interval; NR, Not reporte; NA, Not available.a
At least one year of primary care data prior to diagnosis.b
At least 2 years of primary care data available prior to diagnosis.

While recurrent abdominal pain was the most reported symptom (several cancer sites), it had a PPV ≥3% in only one study for colorectal cancer.18 In contrast, recurrent rectal bleeding, an alarm symptom, was shown to have a PPV ≥3% in all but one34 of the four colorectal cancer studies where it was investigated, across different age groups (Table 3). Recurrent abdominal pain and rectal bleeding were shown to have PPVs ≥3% in age groups lower than currently recommended for testing in NICE NG12 (Fig. 1). Recurrent abdominal distension, although included in NG12 as a persistent symptom for consideration of testing, showed PPV of 4.3% for women aged ≥40 years olds while NG12 highlights testing in women especially ≥50 years old.Fig. 1
Recurrent symptoms with Positive Predictive Values (PPVs) ≥3% reported by included studies. y, years of age; PPV, positive predictive value. ∗Bleeding consists of: colorectal bleeding, melaena, gastrointestinal bleeding and unclassified bleeding type.

The confidence intervals (CIs) for several symptom-specific PPV estimates were broad. Of 11 symptoms with reported CIs, only three (for recurrent visible haematuria, post-menopausal bleeding and dysphagia) had CIs which did not have a lower range that crossed the 3% PPV threshold25 (Fig. 1).

sec.title: Persistent symptoms
sec.p: Data on symptom persistence were available in seven studies,16,22,23,27,28,31,35 conducted in secondary (n = 5)16,22,23,31,35 and primary care (n = 2).27,28 Four were carried out in the UK,23,27,28,35 and one each in Canada,31 Denmark16 and Iran.22 They reported on colorectal cancer,31 head and neck cancer,23,35 lymphoma,27,28 soft tissue and bone sarcoma,16 and upper GI cancers.22 In five studies, patients had been referred to secondary care for further investigations (fast-track/urgent referral or other routes). The remaining two studies analysed primary care records using a case–control design. Five symptoms (oral/lips mass or swelling, oral mucosa ulceration, neck lump/mass, any mass code, dysphagia and hoarseness) were reported in four studies, two about head and neck cancers,23,35 and two about lymphoma.27,28 The remaining symptoms were only reported in one study (Supplementary Table S5).

Symptom information was collected from interviews,22 surveys,31 record reviews,16,35 medical codes (primary care)27,28 and audit.23 The definition of symptom persistence varied across study designs, data sources and by symptoms. Three studies (using interviews, audit and record review) used duration (days or weeks) as a proxy for persistence,22,23,35 one survey study used the proportion of time the symptom presented (25%, 50%, 75% or 100% of the time),31 one did not qualify the term ‘persistent’,16 and two studies using coded data from primary care records used two mass codes at least 42 days or six weeks apart as a proxy for persistent symptoms (Supplementary Table S6).27,28

PPVs ranged from 0% (hard stools 100% of the time amongst those ≥16 years; colorectal cancer)31 to 100% (tooth mobility not associated with periodontal disease for head and neck cancer).35 As expected with patients who had already been referred for investigations, most symptoms had a PPV ≥3% for cancer for the studies performed in secondary care (Fig. 2). With the exception of the Simpkins 2017 study,31 where stool consistency and frequency were categorised into percentage time present, PPVs for colorectal cancer were reported to be under 3% for most reported patterns of diarrhoea or constipation (Supplementary Table S5). In the two primary care studies, PPVs ≥3% were reported for any two mass codes at least six weeks apart (for Hodgkin lymphoma; ages ≥40 years),27 and any two mass codes at least six weeks apart (non-Hodgkin lymphoma; ages 40–59 years).28Fig. 2
Persistent symptoms with positive predictive values (PPVs) ≥3% reported by included studies. CNS, Central Nervous System; GI, gastrointestinal; H&N, Head and Neck; NHL, Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma; y, years of age; PPV, positive predictive value. ∗Shephard 2015b is the only study carried out in primary care. All other studies refer to secondary care populations.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review identified 129 PPVs for 33 recurrent and 22 persistent symptoms across 21 studies which included nearly 200,000 patients. Lower and upper GI cancers dominated the evidence base. PPVs for symptom recurrence were only reported in the primary care setting with 36/85 (42%) relating to an alarm symptom—recurrent jaundice had the highest PPV (31.6%) for pancreatic cancer. Four non-alarm recurrent symptoms (abdominal pain, distension, urinary frequency/urgency and nocturia) reported PPV ≥3% for colorectal, ovarian and prostate cancer respectively. Notably, recurrent abdominal distension, abdominal pain and rectal bleeding in patients at age groups younger than currently stipulated in NICE NG12 have also been found to have PPVs of ≥3% in some studies. Evidence on persistent symptoms were often documented in secondary care settings (5/7 studies), with overall higher PPVs compared to evidence on symptom recurrence.

Although our findings substantiate existing global literature on the high PPVs of many alarm symptoms for cancer in primary care, there is a paucity of evidence on cancer risk in patients with recurrent and persistent symptoms beyond alarm symptoms.4,38 Additionally, these concepts are not well-defined in relation to cancer symptoms. Current NICE NG12 guidelines relating to ovarian cancer specify persistent symptoms (abdominal distension, early satiety/appetite loss, abdominal pain or urinary urgency/frequency) as more than 12 times per month.39 In the Simpkins paper, we also found variations in the way frequency was characterised for bowel habits (e.g. number of times per week, proportion of time symptom was present).31 Therefore, persistence may be defined based on symptom frequency and duration, but also more broadly as mentioned previously. Although symptom characteristics such as number, pattern, frequency and severity have been used to classify ongoing symptoms in the literature around medically unexplained symptoms,12 this information is likely to vary with different symptoms in the context of cancer.

In our broader review (also including combinations of symptoms and cancer risk), we found increasing evidence on abdominal pain in recent years, in particular relating to the cancer risk of the symptom in combination with other features or blood tests, for single and multi-site cancers.40,41 However, evidence on recurrent or persistent abdominal pain is scarce. This review highlights the limited evidence on cancer risk for recurrent and persistent symptoms in different patient subgroups and describe those which may not be included in current NICE guidelines. In particular, increasing evidence on the significance of some symptoms for cancer risk in younger patients for colorectal cancer aligns with the need to improve early diagnosis in this group due to rising global incidence.42

This review has a comprehensive scope, encompasses recurrent and persistent symptoms across a wide spectrum of cancer sites and healthcare settings. Having multiple independent reviewers and the rigorous use of QUADAS-2 enhance confidence in study selection, data extraction and critical appraisal. The focus on PPVs directly addresses the clinical decision-making context faced by GPs.

The breadth of the review resulted in significant heterogeneity in study designs and populations, precluding meta-analysis. Although 14 cancer sites were reported overall, relevant findings for each cancer site were mostly reported by one study only, limiting the generalisability of the findings to a different context and age groups which were not studied. Furthermore, CIs for several symptom-specific PPVs were broad with many crossing the 3% threshold for referral.

The predominance of case–control designs (76% of studies) raises the risk of selection bias as patients are not recruited consecutively nor randomly allocated to groups. Furthermore, as cases and controls often do not receive the same reference standard (or this information is not available) there is the risk of misclassification. Without information on the reference standard, it is not possible to assess whether an appropriate test was used to diagnose cancer, and whether the interval between the reference standard and symptom recording was adequate (hence the unclear results for flow and timing, Table 2). Case-control designs are more appropriate to answer questions about diagnostic test potential, in initial stages of a research study, and less efficient to assess diagnostic accuracy in specific clinical settings.43 Due to selection of symptomatic cancer cases and controls these designs have also been shown to overestimate the PPVs when the same tests are applied to clinical groups showing a different spectrum of the disease (spectrum bias).

These are well-recognised challenges when collecting large-scale population data in primary care. Further, changes in access to investigations and referral pathways over time may limit comparability across studies. Despite the study design limitations, many of the seminal case–control studies identified here have influenced referral guidelines in the UK and worldwide. Increasing number of more recent, prospective studies may update some of the earlier evidence, although the focus of these studies has been on non-specific symptoms, singly or in combination with other clinical features, rather than recurrent or persistent symptoms. More prospective studies, with lower risk of selection bias, misclassification and spectrum bias, will help to generate a stronger body of evidence to inform clinical practice and clinical guidelines.

As with all data using electronic health records, the reliance on coded data may introduce bias as GPs may selectively record alarm symptoms or the most predominant symptom whilst omitting non-specific or non-alarm features. This could systematically underestimate the utility of non-alarm symptoms. Further, coded data introduce limitations in defining recurrence and persistence, as duration and frequency are not typically available and may be hard to operationalise. More fundamentally, coded symptom data represent symptoms as documented by clinicians following consultations rather than standardised patient-reported outcome measures. Consequently, symptom coding may not fully capture patient-reported severity, duration or impact; consultation dynamics and patient characteristics (e.g. help-seeking behaviour, communication style, previous attendance patterns) that influence both symptom reporting and GP interpretation. Finally, the character and quality of symptoms, which may be crucial to clinical decision-making, are lost in binary present/absent coding.

sec.title: Implications and future directions
sec.p: We found inconsistences in the definitions of recurrent symptoms when considering cancer risk. Although many studies considered repeat attendances with the same symptoms, there is a lack of evidence on the time interval between presentations and number of episodes deemed significant enough to warrant further investigations. Most studies also did not systematically capture symptom duration or severity. We consider this to be a key limitation of the current evidence and a major barrier to clinical translation. For example, this ambiguity is reflected in the current NICE NG12, which recommends that GPs consider further investigations for ‘unexplained’ recurrent symptoms for some cancer sites (bladder, leukaemia, lung) without specifying the number of episodes or time interval between recurrent events, required duration, or severity thresholds.39 This ambiguity may contribute to delays in referrals and diagnosis for cancer patients, such as in those with recurrent urinary tract infections and subsequent bladder cancer.44

Beyond alarm symptoms currently included in NG12, we found three recurrent symptom–age combinations for which PPVs were ≥3% for colorectal cancer but not included in some current national guidelines: recurrent abdominal pain in those aged ≥40 years (3.0%), recurrent bleeding in those aged ≥18 years (5.4%), and recurrent bleeding in patients aged ≥40 years (6.8%). Excluding the Scottish guidelines (which does not specify an age threshold for referral), these criteria are currently not included in national referrals guidelines in Denmark, England and Ireland for those aged 40–49 years. For example, the English NG12 guidelines recommend further investigations (with a faecal immunochemical test (FIT)) for those aged ≥50 years with “unexplained abdominal pain or rectal bleeding”.42 Further, non-cancer specific symptoms such as recurrent abdominal pain may also be a signal for colorectal cancer in those under 50 years old. We recommend more research in younger symptomatic patients to inform future guideline development. Given the existing widespread availability of FIT in many European countries, it is worth considering colorectal cancer and therefore a FIT when younger patients present with recurrent abdominal pain or rectal bleeding.

Although NG12 specifies that persistent abdominal distension should be further investigated for ovarian cancer especially in women aged ≥50 years, our findings suggest that recurrent abdominal distension in women aged ≥40 years may also indicate the need for primary care testing. Recent evidence suggests that primary care testing with Cancer Antigen 125 (CA-125) using age-specific thresholds may be a cost-effective way to triage women with suspected ovarian cancer for specialist referral.45 The discordance between existing evidence and NICE recommendations on the use of ‘persistent’ and ‘recurrent’ symptoms reflects the challenges in defining these concepts. Lastly, evidence on recurring symptoms beyond two presentations was limited.

There were inconsistent definitions of persistence and limited primary care evidence except for two primary care studies which operationalised persistence by identifying two mass codes at least six weeks apart.27,28 Nevertheless, guidelines have defined persistence for different symptoms. For example, the Scottish Cancer Referral guidelines define abdominal pain as four weeks in the context of colorectal cancer,8 and Irish guidelines defines persistent rectal bleeding as more than six weeks.7 In general, evidence on cancer risk of persistent symptoms lack validation in the community setting where most cancer presentations first occur, suggesting that more prospective, large-scale population studies are needed to inform guidance on managing ongoing symptoms.

Our findings provide the evidence base to support the development of interventions which can either improve the identification of at-risk patients (such as electronic alerts for patients with recurrent or persistent symptoms), or the development of clinical decision support tools which incorporate symptom recurrence and persistence as risk factors. Future high-quality studies that address the evidence gaps in definitions and prospectively validate symptom timing, frequency and severity can also help refine guidelines.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p:
Beyond classic site-specific alarm symptoms, our findings suggest that recurrent abdominal distension, abdominal pain and rectal bleeding in primary care may warrant further investigations in patients younger than the current age thresholds included in some European guidelines. For researchers, the priorities are validation of cancer risk for recurrent and persistent symptoms for different (and younger) age groups in prospective designs, and improving the consistency in defining these concepts for different symptoms and cancer sites. Future high-quality evidence will refine current guidelines that aim to identify patients at risk of cancer who do not have alarm symptoms, facilitate earlier cancer diagnosis and ultimately improving outcomes for them.Search strategy and selection criteria
This systematic review followed PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses) 2020 guidelines46 and was registered in PROSPERO (The International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews; CRD42024508948). We searched Medline, Embase and the Cochrane library for primary research without date or language restrictions from inception (1946) to November 11, 2024. The search strategy was informed by search filter resources,47,48 and other systematic reviews.49, 50, 51, 52 Search strategies were tested to ensure key relevant papers were being identified.26,53,54
Search strategies included keywords and subject headings (MeSH and EMTREE) relating to: diagnostic performance measures, cancer, symptoms, and recurrence/persistence/combination/repeat attendance (Supplementary File S1). Our search strategies were developed to identify both symptom combinations (to be reported in a separate manuscript) and symptom recurrence or persistence (the focus of this manuscript).
We included peer-reviewed primary research that: a) reported on at least one quantitative measure of diagnostic performance for cancer; b) was conducted in symptomatic patients presenting to healthcare settings (primary or secondary care); and c) reported on either the diagnostic value of symptom combinations, or recurrent or persistent symptoms. We did not pre-define recurrent or persistent symptoms and included studies which described them as such, to better understand how these terms are reported in the literature. Articles reporting on symptom prevalence in asymptomatic general population cohorts, recurrent or metastatic cancer, predictive value of single (non-recurring/persisting) symptoms, symptoms within risk prediction models exclusively, or those with a total sample size under 50 were excluded. We did not restrict the studies based on care setting so we could capture studies and health systems where primary and secondary care differentiation was unclear.
Two reviewers (NC and YZ) independently searched the databases. After de-duplication, titles and abstracts were screened independently by two reviewers (any of AA, FD, NC, YZ), followed by independent (any two of AA, AC, AV, FD, MM, NC, YZ) full-text screening. Backward and forward citation searches of included studies were performed, followed by titles and abstracts screening (AA, AC, NC) and independent full-text review (any two of AA, AC, AV, FD, MM, NC, YZ). Disagreements were discussed and resolved with a third co-author.

sec.title: Contributors
sec.p: YZ and NC conceptualised the study, developed and refined the search strategy. FD, NC, AA, AC, AV, MM and YZ screened articles, extracted data and critically appraised the included studies. FD, NC and YZ wrote the first draft of the manuscript. AA, AC, MM and FW provided critical review of the manuscript. FD, NC and YZ had full access to all the data in the study, and had final responsibility for the decision to submit for publication.

sec.title: Declaration of interests
sec.p: We declare no competing interests.

ack.title: Acknowledgements

The views expressed are those of the author(s) and not necessarily those of the NIHR or the 10.13039/501100000276Department of Health and Social Care.
